note: Stable knowledge-layer artifact for transferable thinking models. Patterns are conceptual recognition models, not algorithm-name tags and not learner progress. New patterns should be rare and require a genuinely new transferable way of thinking; otherwise link new problems to an existing pattern.

PAT-001.name: Maintaining an Invariant
PAT-001.mental_model: Define what a running state means after each processed element, then make every update preserve that meaning.
recognition_signals: A running variable must mean something precise after each index
Correctness depends on what remains true after every update
The implementation can be derived from the state definition
PAT-001.core_invariant: After processing index i, each tracked state exactly represents the best valid value for the subproblem ending at or observed through i.
PAT-001.proof_idea: Prove the invariant is true at initialization, then show each update preserves it for the next index.
PAT-001.complexity_reasoning: Each element is processed once and each update is constant work, so the scan is linear with constant auxiliary state.
common_mistakes: Using an initial value that does not satisfy the invariant
Updating a variable before preserving the previous state it depends on
Explaining intuition without stating the maintained truth

PAT-002.name: Running Extremum Reset
PAT-002.mental_model: Carry a running best value only while it helps future choices; when the carried state becomes harmful, restart from the current element.
recognition_signals: A contiguous segment can be extended or restarted
A negative or worse prefix can only hurt future results
The answer may occur before the final index
PAT-002.core_invariant: The running state is the best non-empty value ending at the current index, while the global answer is the best value seen anywhere so far.
PAT-002.proof_idea: At each index the optimal ending segment either extends the previous optimal ending segment or starts at the current element; no other segment ending here is better.
PAT-002.complexity_reasoning: The recurrence compares a constant number of choices per index, so the pass is O(n) with O(1) extra space.
common_mistakes: Initializing with zero when the answer must be a real non-empty segment
Returning the running state instead of the global answer
Restarting based on the current value instead of the previous carried state

PAT-003.name: Two Competing States
PAT-003.mental_model: When a future operation can flip best and worst, carry both extremes because either can become the answer later.
recognition_signals: Negative values or sign flips change which state is useful
Only tracking the current best loses information
The transition needs values from before the update
PAT-003.core_invariant: At each index, runningMax and runningMin are the maximum and minimum products of non-empty subarrays ending at that index.
PAT-003.proof_idea: The next product can come from the current value, the previous maximum, or the previous minimum; a negative multiplier can swap the roles.
PAT-003.complexity_reasoning: Each index evaluates a fixed set of candidates and stores only two running states plus the answer.
common_mistakes: Forgetting to preserve previous runningMax before updating runningMin
Initializing with artificial infinities instead of the first real element
Returning local runningMax instead of maintaining globalMax

PAT-004.name: Prefix Extremum
PAT-004.mental_model: For each current value, only the best historical prefix candidate matters; all worse historical candidates can be ignored.
recognition_signals: A decision compares the current value with something seen earlier
Only one historical minimum or maximum affects all future answers
The order constraint forbids using future values
PAT-004.core_invariant: The prefix extremum stores the best eligible historical value before or at the current decision point.
PAT-004.proof_idea: If one historical candidate is worse than another for every possible future value, it can never produce a better answer.
PAT-004.complexity_reasoning: The best historical value is updated once per index, replacing the nested scan with one pass.
common_mistakes: Tracking an unnecessary running answer state
Updating the historical value after using an invalid future relation
Missing that order is part of the invariant

PAT-005.name: Local Gain Accumulation
PAT-005.mental_model: When every positive adjacent improvement can be independently captured, summing local gains equals the best global result.
recognition_signals: Multiple non-overlapping opportunities can all be taken
Only positive adjacent changes matter
A global valley-to-peak move decomposes into local positive steps
PAT-005.core_invariant: The accumulated answer equals the sum of all profitable local transitions observed so far.
PAT-005.proof_idea: Any increasing run's total profit is exactly the sum of its positive adjacent differences, so taking each local rise loses nothing.
PAT-005.complexity_reasoning: Each adjacent pair is inspected once and no historical state beyond the answer is needed.
common_mistakes: Reusing the single-transaction invariant after the constraint changes
Looking for one best buy/sell pair when all local gains can be taken
Overcomplicating with unnecessary state

PAT-006.name: Reachability Frontier
PAT-006.mental_model: Track the farthest reachable frontier while scanning; a new index is useful only if it expands the known reachable region.
recognition_signals: Each index offers reach or coverage into the future
The question asks whether or how fast a target can be reached
Brute force repeats reachability checks from overlapping ranges
PAT-006.core_invariant: After scanning an eligible region, the frontier is the farthest index reachable using the decisions allowed so far.
PAT-006.proof_idea: All positions up to the current frontier are reachable, so the best next extension is the maximum reach among positions inside that region.
PAT-006.complexity_reasoning: The scan pointer advances monotonically and each index contributes to the frontier at most once.
contrast_with: Dynamic programming over every prior index
common_mistakes: Confusing the scan pointer with the reachability frontier
Not proving why skipped intermediate choices remain covered
Updating the step boundary at the wrong time

PAT-007.name: Greedy Candidate Elimination
PAT-007.mental_model: If one candidate fails after carrying the strongest possible local state, every candidate inside that failed segment is also invalid.
recognition_signals: Need to choose one starting point or candidate
A failed candidate invalidates a whole range
Brute force retries overlapping candidate ranges
Local feasibility and global feasibility are separate questions
PAT-007.core_invariant: Once cumulative deficit becomes negative, no index inside that failed segment can be a valid starting candidate.
PAT-007.proof_idea: Show that an intermediate candidate starts with no more useful balance than the failed candidate had before the failure point, so it must also fail before or at that point.
PAT-007.complexity_reasoning: The scan never restarts; each index is observed once while candidate changes only update the hypothesis state.
common_mistakes: Restarting the scan pointer instead of changing only the candidate
Confusing local tank balance with global feasibility
Knowing the update rule without the elimination proof

PAT-008.name: Bidirectional Constraint Satisfaction
PAT-008.mental_model: When local validity depends on both left and right neighbors, let each directional pass satisfy one side of the constraint and merge the strongest requirement at each position.
recognition_signals: Each element is constrained by adjacent neighbors on both sides
A left-to-right greedy pass satisfies only the left-neighbor relation
A right-to-left pass is needed to satisfy the opposite relation
The final value must preserve both directional requirements
PAT-008.core_invariant: After each directional pass, all constraints facing that direction are satisfied; taking the maximum per index preserves both directional lower bounds while keeping the standard assignment minimal.
PAT-008.proof_idea: Each child must receive at least the amount required by the left constraint and at least the amount required by the right constraint. The maximum of those two lower bounds is therefore necessary, and assigning exactly that maximum is sufficient.
PAT-008.complexity_reasoning: Each pass scans the array once and the merge/sum scans once, so the standard construction is O(n) time with O(n) extra space.
common_mistakes: Assuming the first directional pass is globally sufficient
Overwriting one directional requirement with the other instead of taking the maximum
Treating the O(1) space optimization as required before mastering the standard two-pass proof

PAT-009.name: Repeated Query to Minimal State
PAT-009.mental_model: When brute force repeatedly asks the same existence, index, count, or grouping question, define the exact question first, then store only the information needed to answer it instantly.
recognition_signals: Brute force repeatedly searches previously seen values
The repeated question is about existence, location, count, or grouping
A direct scan can be replaced by a lookup
The lookup key should match the repeated question
PAT-009.core_invariant: Before processing the current item, the lookup structure contains exactly the processed information needed to answer the repeated query for items already seen.
PAT-009.proof_idea: If every future decision only needs the answer to the repeated query over processed items, storing that query's minimal state preserves correctness while removing the repeated scan.
PAT-009.complexity_reasoning: The loop still performs n iterations, but each repeated query becomes an average O(1) lookup or update, so the total expected time is O(n).
common_mistakes: Choosing a data structure before naming the repeated query
Searching after insertion when the current element must not match itself
Explaining O(n) without separating number of iterations from lookup cost
